prompt_preview:
You are an expert AI teaching assistant creating an educational presentation.

Generate exactly 10 s
response:
{
  "slides": [
       {
           "slide_type": "intro",
           "title": "Introduction to Decision Trees",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Decision trees are a type of supervised machine learning algorithm.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "They work well with features on different scales and with a mix of binary and continuous features.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Decision trees are invariant to scaling of the data, eliminating the need for normalization or standardization.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "They can be used for both classification and regression tasks.", "source_id": "Page 89"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees are a versatile and efficient machine learning algorithm.",
           "visual_hint": "hierarchy",
           "image_id": null,
           "speaker_notes": "Start by introducing the concept of decision trees and their general characteristics."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "definition",
           "title": "Decision Tree Components",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "A decision tree consists of nodes, which represent features or tests.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "Each node has two child nodes, representing the possible outcomes of the test.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The tree is grown recursively, with each node splitting the data into two subsets.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The process stops when all leaves are pure, meaning all instances in a node belong to the same class.", "source_id": "Page 89"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees are composed of nodes and edges, forming a hierarchical structure.",
           "visual_hint": "flowchart",
           "image_id": "IMG_003",
           "speaker_notes": "Explain the basic components of a decision tree and how they are connected."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "concept",
           "title": "How Decision Trees Work",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Decision trees work by recursively partitioning the data into smaller subsets.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "At each node, the algorithm selects the best feature to split the data.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The splitting process continues until all instances in a node belong to the same class.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The tree can be pruned to prevent overfitting and improve generalization performance.", "source_id": "Page 97"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees recursively partition the data into smaller subsets to make predictions.",
           "visual_hint": "process",
           "image_id": null,
           "speaker_notes": "Describe the process of how decision trees work, from splitting to pruning."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "example",
           "title": "Example: Decision Tree on the Breast Cancer Dataset",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "The Breast Cancer dataset is a classic example for decision tree classification.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The dataset is split into a training and test set to evaluate the model's performance.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The decision tree is grown using the training data, and its performance is evaluated on the test data.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The tree can be visualized to understand the decision-making process.", "source_id": "Page 89"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees can be applied to real-world datasets, such as the Breast Cancer dataset.",
           "visual_hint": "none",
           "image_id": "IMG_004",
           "speaker_notes": "Use a concrete example to illustrate how decision trees can be applied to a real-world problem."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "concept",
           "title": "Pre-Pruning and Post-Pruning",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Pre-pruning stops the tree from growing when a stopping criterion is met.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Post-pruning removes branches from a fully grown tree to prevent overfitting.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Pre-pruning is faster but may not always find the optimal tree.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Post-pruning can be more effective but is often more computationally expensive.", "source_id": "Page 97"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Pre-pruning and post-pruning are techniques used to prevent overfitting in decision trees.",
           "visual_hint": "comparison",
           "image_id": null,
           "speaker_notes": "Explain the difference between pre-pruning and post-pruning, and their trade-offs."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "comparison",
           "title": "Decision Trees vs. Random Forests",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Random forests are an ensemble method that combines multiple decision trees.", "source_id": "Page 101"},
               {"text": "Random forests can handle high-dimensional data and are more robust to overfitting.", "source_id": "Page 101"},
               {"text": "Decision trees are more interpretable and can be visualized easily.", "source_id": "Page 101"},
               {"text": "Random forests can be more accurate but may be more difficult to interpret.", "source_id": "Page 101"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees and random forests have different strengths and weaknesses.",
           "visual_hint": "timeline",
           "image_id": "IMG_002",
           "speaker_notes": "Compare and contrast decision trees with random forests, highlighting their respective advantages and disadvantages."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "example",
           "title": "Example: Decision Tree on a Simple Dataset",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "A simple dataset can be used to illustrate the decision-making process of a decision tree.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The dataset is split into a training and test set to evaluate the model's performance.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The decision tree is grown using the training data, and its performance is evaluated on the test data.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "The tree can be visualized to understand the decision-making process.", "source_id": "Page 87"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees can be applied to simple datasets to illustrate their decision-making process.",
           "visual_hint": "mindmap",
           "image_id": "IMG_005",
           "speaker_notes": "Use a simple example to demonstrate how decision trees work and make predictions."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "concept",
           "title": "Handling Missing Values",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Decision trees can handle missing values by using surrogate splits.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Surrogate splits are alternative splits that can be used when a feature is missing.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "The tree can also be grown using only the features that are available for a given instance.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Missing values can be imputed before growing the tree, but this may introduce bias.", "source_id": "Page 97"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees can handle missing values using surrogate splits or other methods.",
           "visual_hint": "hierarchy",
           "image_id": null,
           "speaker_notes": "Explain how decision trees can handle missing values and the potential implications."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "comparison",
           "title": "Decision Trees in scikit-learn",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "scikit-learn implements decision trees in the DecisionTreeRegressor and DecisionTreeClassifier classes.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The classes provide parameters for controlling the tree's growth, such as max_depth and min_samples_split.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "The trees can be visualized using the plot_tree function from scikit-learn.", "source_id": "Page 89"},
               {"text": "scikit-learn only implements pre-pruning, not post-pruning.", "source_id": "Page 89"}
           ],
           "key_message": "scikit-learn provides an implementation of decision trees with various parameters for control.",
           "visual_hint": "process",
           "image_id": "IMG_006",
           "speaker_notes": "Describe the implementation of decision trees in scikit-learn and their features."
       },
       {
           "slide_type": "summary",
           "title": "Summary of Decision Trees",
           "bullets": [
               {"text": "Decision trees are a type of supervised machine learning algorithm.", "source_id": "Page 87"},
               {"text": "They work well with features on different scales and with a mix of binary and continuous features.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Decision trees can be prone to overfitting, but techniques like pre-pruning and post-pruning can help.", "source_id": "Page 97"},
               {"text": "Decision trees are widely used in many applications, including classification and regression tasks.", "source_id": "Page 101"}
           ],
           "key_message": "Decision trees are a powerful and versatile machine learning algorithm with many applications.",
           "visual_hint": "none",
           "image_id": null,
           "speaker_notes": "Summarize the key points of decision trees and their importance in machine learning."
       }
   ]
}
